sections.heading: Wanting to stop rises quickly after the first child
sections.body: Across the Philippines, 57.3 percent of currently married women wanted no more children or were sterilized in 2025, almost the same as 57.6 percent in 2022. The bigger change appears by family size. The share was 26.1 percent among women with one living child, then rose to 63.3 percent among women with two living children. By three living children, 78.4 percent wanted no more children, and the share stayed above 80 percent for women with four or more. In 2025, the clearest turning point was between one and two children, even though the national total changed very little.

sections.heading: Urban and rural totals are almost the same, but urban women reach high levels earlier
sections.body: In 2025, the overall urban-rural gap was small: 57.5 percent of urban women and 57.1 percent of rural women wanted no more children or were sterilized. But urban women were more likely than rural women to want no more children at one child, 29.1 percent versus 22.2 percent, and at two children, 65.2 percent versus 61.0 percent. Among women with six or more living children, the urban share reached 91.8 percent, compared with 87.7 percent in rural areas. The main difference is not the total level, but that urban women tend to reach a strong desire to stop a little earlier as family size grows.

sections.heading: Education gaps get smaller when women have the same number of children
sections.body: Overall, wanting to stop childbearing was highest among women with Grades 1-6 education at 68.1 percent and lowest among women with Grades 11-12 at 23.1 percent. The gap becomes smaller among women with two living children: 59.4 percent of women with Grades 1-6 and 48.8 percent of women with Grades 11-12 wanted no more children. Among women with college education, the two-child level was 65.1 percent, close to the national two-child level of 63.3 percent. This means the education difference is much less visible when women are compared within the same family-size group.

sections.heading: Wealth gaps are small overall, but larger among women with more children
sections.body: In 2025, the total desire to stop childbearing across wealth quintiles ranged from 55.4 percent in the highest quintile to 59.4 percent in the second quintile. That is a small spread at the total level, but the gap is wider for some larger family sizes. Among women with four living children, 94.2 percent in the highest quintile wanted no more children, compared with 79.7 percent in the lowest quintile. The pattern suggests that wealth differences are less about whether women want to stop at all, and more about how strong that preference is once families are already larger.

sections.heading: BARMM is still lowest, even though it improved
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest total desire to limit childbearing in 2025 at 29.7 percent, far below the national level of 57.3 percent. It increased from 22.6 percent in 2022, with gains across several family sizes, including 26.4 percent at two children and 58.8 percent at six or more children in 2025. Even among women with three living children, only 34.9 percent of currently married women in BARMM wanted no more children, compared with 78.4 percent nationally. BARMM is therefore not only low overall; the desire to stop rises later and stays lower at nearly every family size.

sections.heading: Zamboanga Peninsula dropped sharply from 2022
sections.body: Zamboanga Peninsula¹ had one of the clearest regional declines, with the total falling from 58.8 percent in 2022 to 42.7 percent in 2025. The drop is especially clear among women with two and three living children: from 58.3 to 44.6 percent at two children, and from 73.6 to 55.1 percent at three children. The region also stayed low at larger family sizes, including 62.4 percent at four children and 68.4 percent at six or more. It stands out not only because its total is low, but because the desire to stop is weaker across much of the family-size ladder.

sections.heading: Several regions are now above 60 percent overall
sections.body: CAR, MIMAROPA, and Bicol were among the highest regions in 2025, at 63.7, 63.2, and 62.3 percent, respectively. CAR stands out because it rose from 55.3 percent in 2022 and reached 68.4 percent by two living children and 87.2 percent by three. MIMAROPA also had a high total and an early rise, reaching 65.8 percent at two children and 77.7 percent at three. These regions show that high overall levels often come not only from very large families, but also from a strong shift toward stopping by the second or third child.

sections.heading: The national total hides bigger regional changes
sections.body: The national total was nearly flat, but many regions changed more noticeably. Bicol fell from 70.1 percent in 2022 to 62.3 percent in 2025, Western Visayas declined from 65.0 to 58.5 percent, and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ declined from 58.8 to 42.7 percent. In the other direction, BARMM rose from 22.6 to 29.7 percent, CAR rose from 55.3 to 63.7 percent, and Northern Mindanao rose from 54.2 to 59.5 percent. The almost flat national trend therefore hides regional shifts moving in different directions.

takeaways: National desire to limit childbearing was almost unchanged from 2022, but the biggest shift still happens between one and two living children.
BARMM and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ stand out for low levels, while CAR, MIMAROPA, and Bicol are among the highest regions overall.
Urban-rural and wealth differences are modest overall, but family size shows stronger contrasts in when the desire to stop childbearing becomes common.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
